CZ_NAME: S & E JACKSON / LARIMER / N & NE GRAND / NW BOULDER COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought periods of moderate to heavy snow to parts of the North Central Mountains, Front Range Foothills, Urban Corridor with blizzard conditions developing over the parts of the Northeast Plains of Colorado.  The combination of snow and wind reportedly reduced the visibilities to just a few hundred feet at times, and resulted in several road closures.  Across eastern Elbert, Lincoln, Logan, Phillips, Sedgwick and Washington Counties, gusty northerly winds ranged from 35 to 55 mph producing extensive blowing and drifting snow, ranging from 1 to 4 feet in depth.  Storm totals in those areas were difficult to measure but estimated to range from 3 to 5 inches.   ||Road closures included:  Interstate 70, east of Aurora to the Kansas Stateline; U.S. Highway 24, from Limon to Colorado Springs; Colorado Highway 94 between Punkin Center and Enoch; U.S. Highway 40/287 between Limon and Kit Carson; Colorado Highway 71 from east of Fort Morgan to Limon; U.S. Highway 34 from Brush to Yuma.||In the mountain and foothills, the heaviest amounts generally occurred north of Interstate 70.  Storm totals included:  13 inches at Estes Park, 12 inches at Genesee and Red Feather Lakes, 11 inches at Glen Haven; 10.5 inches at Livermore, 9 inches, 4 miles west-southwest of Eldorado Springs; 8.5 inches at Coal Creek Canyon and near Crescent Village; 8 inches near Evergreen, 7 inches at Drake and Bear Lake; with 6 inches at Eldora Ski Area, Idaho Springs, Grandby, Gross Reservoir and Nederland.|Along the Urban Corridor and adjacent plains, lesser storm totals from 1 to 4 inches were observed.

A broad Pacific trough brought snowfall to the higher elevations and to some lower elevation areas of northwest Colorado.  The upper trough also ushered into the region some of the coldest air of the season so far.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread snowfall accumulations of 12 to 17 inches were measured across the area, including 17 inches on Rabbit Ears Pass where the chain law was put into effect.  Wind gusts of 20 to 40 mph produced areas of blowing and drifting snow.

CZ_NAME: GORE AND ELK MOUNTAINS/CENTRAL MOUNTAIN VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad Pacific trough brought snowfall to the higher elevations and to some lower elevation areas of northwest Colorado.  The upper trough also ushered into the region some of the coldest air of the season so far.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulations of 5 to 13 inches were measured across the area.  Wind gusts of 20 to 40 mph produced areas of blowing and drifting snow.  I-70 at Vail Pass was closed for a period of time due to the inclement weather.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall resulted in a flash flood across a section of Highway 133.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall resulted in a flash flood across a section of Highway 133 about six miles south of Carbondale. The flash flood deposited mud and debris up to three feet deep on the highway. Highway 133 was closed for nearly three hours due to this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous strong to severe thunderstorms moved through western Colorado on the afternoon of July 14th, producing areas of very heavy rainfall and wind gusts over 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall 5 miles west of Glenwood Springs produced mudslides which closed one lane of west bound Interstate 70.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historically low snow pack over the Colorado mountains and persistent below normal precipitation through the spring and early summer months allowed the drought to continue strengthen and expand across western Colorado through June. High wildland fire danger, agricultural impacts and water shortages and restrictions were all being realized by the end of this month across many portions of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought conditions expanded across the majority of the northern Colorado mountains and adjacent plateaus.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm dropped over a third of an inch in under a half of an hour, and produced some small hail as it moved through the city of Gunnison on the afternoon of the 28th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system affected eastern Colorado, far northwest Kansas and parts of southwest Nebraska with strong winds during the day.  Several counties also received one to three inches of snow, which made for near blizzard conditions when combined with the wind.  Interstate 70 was closed from Goodland, Kansas west through much of eastern Colorado.  A 63 mph wind gust was reported at a mesonet site northeast of Kit Carson in Cheyenne County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several inches of snow combined with wind gusts over 50 mph reduced visibility in blowing snow to one quarter to one half mile at times for much of the morning, mainly on east-west roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved across the area, generating southwest winds with gusts of 50 to 60 mph.  In addition blowing dirt reduced visibility below one mile at times.

Across eastern Elbert, Lincoln, Logan, Phillips, Sedgwick and Washington Counties, gusty northerly winds ranged from 35 to 55 mph producing extensive blowing and drifting snow, ranging from 1 to 4 feet in depth.  Storm totals in those areas were difficult to measure but estimated to range from 3 to 5 inches.   ||Road closures included:  Interstate 70, east of Aurora to the Kansas Stateline; U.S. Highway 24, from Limon to Colorado Springs; Colorado Highway 94 between Punkin Center and Enoch; U.S. Highway 40/287 between Limon and Kit Carson; Colorado Highway 71 from east of Fort Morgan to Limon; U.S. Highway 34 from Brush to Yuma.||In the mountain and foothills, the heaviest amounts generally occurred north of Interstate 70.  Storm totals included:  13 inches at Estes Park, 12 inches at Genesee and Red Feather Lakes, 11 inches at Glen Haven; 10.5 inches at Livermore, 9 inches, 4 miles west-southwest of Eldorado Springs; 8.5 inches at Coal Creek Canyon and near Crescent Village; 8 inches near Evergreen, 7 inches at Drake and Bear Lake; with 6 inches at Eldora Ski Area, Idaho Springs, Grandby, Gross Reservoir and Nederland.|Along the Urban Corridor and adjacent plains, lesser storm totals from 1 to 4 inches were observed.

A large winter storm system moved from the Central Rockies across the Central High Plains, producing a large band of four to nine inches of snow.  The storm system initially began as rain in most areas, and changed to snow as temperatures cooled.  Most snow fell along and north of Interstate 70, with areas south of the Interstate receiving a trace to three inches of snow.  Snow totals across Yuma county included eight inches at Wray and Yuma, and seven inches at Joes, Vernon and Idalia.  Reports in Kit Carson county included six inches at Flagler and Stratton and five inches in Burlington.

A strong low pressure system affected eastern Colorado, far northwest Kansas and parts of southwest Nebraska with strong winds during the day.  Several counties also received one to three inches of snow, which made for near blizzard conditions when combined with the wind.  Interstate 70 was closed from Goodland, Kansas west through much of eastern Colorado.  A 63 mph wind gust was reported at a mesonet site northeast of Kit Carson in Cheyenne County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report was from a mesonet site located 9 miles NNE of Kit Carson.

CZ_NAME: WEST JACKSON & WEST GRAND COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two men, on foot, triggered and were caught in a small avalanche. The avalanche was 2 to 3 feet deep, 65 feet wide, and ran 140 feet vertically.   One man was fully buried and the other partially buried. It took some time for the survivor to free himself, he then then had to dig through 5 feet of snow to reach his brother, who was unresponsive when found.||The accident, near Buffalo Pass, occurred on a north aspect below treeline as the two men, on foot, worked their way toward a lower elevation.  The men had gotten stuck snowmobiling prior to the avalanche, and were trying to walk back to the Grizzly Creek campsite.  The second survived two nights in the field and was recovered by friends, family, and rescue personnel on January 23rd.  He was hospitalized and assessed for frostbite.

A deepening surface low over Eastern Colorado caused the surface pressure gradient to increase resulting in strong to severe wind gusts during the afternoon.  During the evening showers and thunderstorms moved into Northwest Kansas.  The outflow winds generated from the showers and thunderstorms moved into Eastern Colorado, enhanced by the already strong winds above the surface, which lead to widespread reports of wind gusts of 60 across the area. The wind gusts peaked during the late evening into the overnight hours then gradually diminished from west to east through the rest of the night.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through East Central Colorado during the early afternoon.  Behind the front winds quickly strengthened, with a peak gust of 67 MPH measured at Cheyenne Wells.   As snow began to fall shortly behind the front, the strong winds and blowing snow lead to visibilities of a quarter mile or less, with some locations reporting white-out conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Burlington airport reported visibilities of a quarter mile or less for 3 hours, with a peak gust of 51 MPH.  Snowfall totals ranged from 2.0 inches at Stratton to 4.5 inches at Burlington.  SH between Seibert and Kit Carson was closed due to poor visibilities.  Both directions of I-70 were closed through the entire county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms produced heavy rainfall of 1 to 3 inches across Black Forest and northern Colorado Springs.  A drainage channel was heavily damaged by the fast-flowing high water.

A strong cold front moved through East Central Colorado during the early afternoon.  Behind the front winds quickly strengthened, with a peak gust of 67 MPH measured at Cheyenne Wells.   As snow began to fall shortly behind the front, the strong winds and blowing snow lead to visibilities of a quarter mile or less, with some locations reporting white-out conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Included Yuma county based on observations from the Akron ASOS and St. Francis AWOS.  An emergency manager at Yuma and a spotter near Joes reported visibilities near zero.  A peak gust of 52 MPH was reported at the Wray AWOS.  Snowfall totals ranged from a trace 6 NW of Liberty and 19 NNE of Wray, to 6.2 inches 4 NNE Vernon.  Road SH 34 between Yuma and Otis was closed and SH 385 between Wray and SH36 was also closed due to poor visibility.

CZ_NAME: GORE AND ELK MOUNTAINS/CENTRAL MOUNTAIN VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Significant snowfall and strong winds resulted in an unstable snowpack just prior to this deadly avalanche.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five skiers entered the Prima Cornice area inside the Vail Ski Area boundary where unstable snow conditions were in place. Three of the skiers traversed a slope and were caught in a 200 foot-wide soft-slab avalanche which began at the 10,800 foot level and ran 400 vertical feet through a forested area on a 46 degree slope.  Two skiers were partially buried and another tumbled outside of the debris field. One of the partially buried skiers died from injuries sustained in the avalanche.

CZ_NAME: GORE AND ELK MOUNTAINS/CENTRAL MOUNTAIN VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A recent storm with significant snowfall and strong winds resulted in a soft slab layer of snow on top of an older unstable snowpack.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small avalanche was triggered in a nnarrow gully by a skier. A second skier become stuck in the initial avalanche debris field and triggered another avalanche when trying to extricate himself. This skier was then buried by the second avalanche in a terrain trap where he was buried for approximately 10 minutes before being uncovered by other skiers.  This fatal avalanche occurred in the Burnt Mountain area outside the boundaries of Snowmass Ski Area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shallow, early snowpack and dry start to January created a layer of weak faceted snow near ground level. Over 8 feet of new snowfall through mid January into February which produced a dense slab over the weaker faceted snow and created conditions favorable for avalanches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large group of skiers were touring the backcountry near Red Mountain pass when one skier triggered an avalanche at the 10,520 foot level. This skier was not caught by the avalanche but two others were caught and buried by the avalanche which deposited avalanche debris up to ten feet deep along the creek bottom. Both skiers where located within 5 minutes, with one buried two feet beneath the snow and the other buried six feet under the snow.  Both skiers survived without injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early season snowfall and a dry start to January created a weak snow layer which was covered by seven feet of newer snow which accumulated from mid January to February 13th. This later snow formed a dense slab over the weak base and create favorable avalanche conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowboarder traveling alone was caught in an avalanche which initiated at the 10,300 foot level and carried the victim downhill 925 vertical feet.  The victim had an avalanche beacon and was found buried under four feet of snow and debris. The avalanche occurred in an area south of the town of Telluride.

CZ_NAME: GORE AND ELK MOUNTAINS/CENTRAL MOUNTAIN VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shallow, early snowpack and dry start to January created a layer of weak faceted snow near ground level. New snowfall through late January into February produced a dense slab over the weaker faceted snow which created conditions favorable for avalanches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trio of skiers entered Mushroom Bowl near Vail Ski Area.  One skier jumped off a 30 foot cliff onto the snow below and triggered an avalanche which started at the 11,450 foot level.  The avalanche path was 100 feet wide and 300 vertical feet. The skier who initiated the avalanche was swept off his feet and carried into a group of small trees, where he received non-life threatening injuries, then became fully buried under one to two feet of snow from a secondary avalanche. The other skiers where able to dig him out then self evacuate from the avalanche site.

CZ_NAME: GORE AND ELK MOUNTAINS/CENTRAL MOUNTAIN VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent moist and strong northwest flow with embedded disturbances produced heavy snowfall in the mountains and some lower elevation areas of northwest Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulations of 8 to 15 inches were measured above the 10,000 foot level, with lesser amounts at lower elevations. Wind gusts of 40 to 55 mph produced localized blizzard conditions.  Locally stronger wind gusts included 74 mph at the Crown RAWS site. I-70 at Vail Pass was closed for several hours due to the inclement weather conditions.

Temperatures near Ophis Pass on March 30th rose above freezing at the 13,000 foot level and were the warmest readings of the year so far.  March 30th was preceded by an uncharacteristically warm and dry period which created favorable avalanche conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A skier in a group of four decided to ski down a southeast aspect near Ophir Pass during a sunny and relatively warm afternoon. This skier initiated a wet slab avalanche that broke loose at about the 12,800 foot level and traveled a distance of over one mile with a path width of up to 500 feet. The skier was carried by the avalanche a total distance of about 4200 feet down a vertical elevation drop of about 1500 feet. Although the skier had an avalanche beacon, he was not found and uncovered from the avalanche debris until about 35 minutes after he was buried.

CZ_NAME: TELLER COUNTY / RAMPART RANGE ABOVE 7500 FT / PIKES PEAK BETWEEN 7500 & 11000 FT
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire erupted in Waldo Canyon, west of Colorado Springs midday June 23rd and was not contained until July 10th.  The fire consumed 18,247 acres, most of which was in National Forest land.  During the afternoon of June 26th, the wildfire exhibited extreme fire behavior during Red Flag conditions.  An outflow boundary from thunderstorms to the northwest, hit the wildfire and drove it down the mountainside into northwest Colorado Springs.  Winds gusted to around 65 mph with the fire storm, which consumed 347 structures and took the lives of a husband and wife in one of the houses.  At the peak, 32,000 people were evacuated from their houses for a number of days.  The President declared Colorado Springs a major disaster area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to severe drought conditions covered all of western Colorado by the end of May, with extreme drought conditions developing across the northwest portion of the state. A historically reduced snow pack and resultant below normal runoff, below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures all contributed to the increase in elevated drought conditions across the region. An increase in wildland fire danger was the main impact from the drought conditions in the late Spring period.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Delayed report received from BNSF railroad insurance agent.  Twenty railroad cars (6 box cars, 14 container cars carrying double-stacked trailers) were blown off the tracks from a thunderstorm downburst around 445 pm MDT. All cars were laying to the north.  No damage was reported in Eckley, just east of the incident.  Train was parked at the time. No mesonet data; peak wind is an estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable snowpack in the Snowmass area resulted in an avalanche that killed a person.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Snowmass ski patroller triggered a portion of the crown from a previous avalanche within the Snowmass Ski Area. The previous avalanche was triggered with explosives on December 27, 2012, three days before the accident. Although the December 30th avalanche was considered relatively small in size, it was large enough to sweep the skier over a cliff.  The time of the fatal avalanche was estimated, and the actual event likely occurred within an hour of the estimate.  A snowfall total of 18 inches was reported from Snowmass Ski Area within four days prior to the December 27th avalanche.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A snowboarder triggered an avalanche which swept him down the side of a mountain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A pair of snowboarders descended a north aspect slope near South Lookout Peak in the Ophir Pass area. As one snowboarder watched from below, the other triggered an avalanche during his descent and was caught in the debris flow and injured as he was carried over two rock cliffs. Some of his gear was damaged, including his helmet.  The injured snowboarder was able to travel out of the back country with the assistance of the other snowboarder and another group in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to severe drought conditions covered all of western Colorado by the end of May, with extreme drought conditions developing across the northwest portion of the state. A historically reduced snow pack and resultant below normal runoff, below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures all contributed to the increase in elevated drought conditions across the region. An increase in wildland fire danger was the main impact from the drought conditions in the late Spring period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions persisted across the upper Gunnison River Valley due to a reduced snow pack and continued below normal precipitation through the spring.

CZ_NAME: NORTHERN EL PASO COUNTY / MONUMENT RIDGE / RAMPART RANGE BELOW 7500 FT
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire erupted in Waldo Canyon, west of Colorado Springs midday June 23rd and was not contained until July 10th.  The fire consumed 18,247 acres, most of which was in National Forest land.  During the afternoon of June 26th, the wildfire exhibited extreme fire behavior during Red Flag conditions.  An outflow boundary from thunderstorms to the northwest, hit the wildfire and drove it down the mountainside into northwest Colorado Springs.  Winds gusted to around 65 mph with the fire storm, which consumed 347 structures and took the lives of a husband and wife in one of the houses.  At the peak, 32,000 people were evacuated from their houses for a number of days.  The President declared Colorado Springs a major disaster area.

CZ_NAME: N DOUGLAS COUNTY BELOW 6000 FEET / DENVER / W ADAMS & ARAPAHOE COUNTIES / E BROOMFIELD COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: June 2012 was the hottest June in Denver since weather records began back in 1872.  There were a total of seventeen 90 degree days in the month of June.  The highlight of month was a stretch of five consecutive 100 degree days from the 22nd to the 26th.  This was only the third time in Denver weather history in which this happened.  Two of the high temperatures during the stretch peaked at 105 degrees, which set the all time record for the month of June and also tied the all time maximum temperature for Denver.
A severe thunderstorm produced hail up to quarter size in Phillips County.

A line of thunderstorms moved northeast across the high plateaus and valleys of northwest Colorado. Brief heavy rain and gusty winds were mainly observed as these storms passed.  However, wind gusts over 60 mph were reported near Meeker.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Outflow wind from a passing thunderstorm produced a wind gust near 60 mph at the Pinto RAWS observation station in central Rio Blanco County.

CZ_NAME: S & E JACKSON / LARIMER / N & NE GRAND / NW BOULDER COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Damaging winds developed in and near the Front Range.  A peak wind gust to 104 mph was recorded in the foothills of Boulder County.  In Boulder, the high winds knocked down several trees, power poles and electrical lines.  Some of the fallen trees damaged homes and automobiles.  A semi-trailer was blown on its side along State Highway 93 near Marshall.  In Loveland, the strong winds downed power lines and caused scattered electrical outages, which affected approximately 150 residents.  In the mountains, the combination light to moderate snow driven by high winds, produced blizzard conditions above timberline.  Storm totals generally ranged from 3 to 8 inches.||Peak wind gusts included:  104 mph in south Boulder; 98 mph, 3 miles southwest of Pinecliffe; 95 mph, 2 miles northwest of Rocky Flats; 92 mph, along State Highway 93 near Marshall; 87 mph atop Berthoud Pass and in Boulder Canyon;  80 mph, 5 miles west-northwest of Boulder; 83 mph at NCAR Mesa Lab; 78 mph, 8 miles northeast of Four Corners; 79 mph at the National Wind Technology Center; 76 mph at Wondervu; 75 mph atop Loveland Pass and the NCAR Foothills Lab in Boulder; 74 mph at Blue Mountain, Boulder Municipal Airport, 9 miles east of Dillon and 1 mile northwest of Lyons; 73 mph, 4 miles east-northeast of Nederland; 72 mph at the Junction of State Highways 72 and 93; 64 mph in Loveland; 62 mph, 6 miles west-northwest of Berthoud and Superior; 61 mph at Erie Municipal Airport and the CSU Campus in Fort Collins; and 60 mph, 10 miles northeast of Pawnee Buttes.

CZ_NAME: GORE AND ELK MOUNTAINS/CENTRAL MOUNTAIN VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: October 2012 continued the string of below normal precipitation months across many of the climate stations in Western Colorado. The valley locations were short of normal of monthly normal precipitation by 0.75 to 1.25 over the majority of the locations while mountain stations were around 0.50 in deficit. For the most part severe to extreme drought persisted across the region with only a temporary improvement to moderate drought conditions over the central Colorado mountains at the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions remained in place over the central Colorado mountain ranges  through the month of October. However, the eastern portion of this zone did recover slightly to moderate drought levels by the last part of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As western Colorado transitioned from fall to winter the storm track became more favorable for precipitation across the eastern Great Basin and northern Rockies, especially during the latter half of the month. Half of the climate reporting systems over western Colorado collected above normal precipitation for the first time in many months. The central and southern mountain ranges and adjacent valleys however continued to receive below normal precipitation through November.  Overall the precipitation was not enough to ease drought conditions and little change was seen in the severe to extreme categories in place across western Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought conditions remained unchanged across the central Yampa River basin and the northwest Colorado plateau region through December.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The trend of monthly below normal precipitation continued into the month of September across western Colorado. All climate reporting stations across the western half of the state reported readings of around a half to an inch below normal with little help from a weakening monsoonal flow. Little change in drought conditions were noted with severe to extreme drought levels remaining in place throughout the month. The wildland fire danger remained high during September but the main impacts were felt in the agricultural sector due to crop and pasture land losses.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: September drought conditions remained unchanged across the Uncompahgre Plateau with severe drought found across the southern portion, including the Dallas Divide area, and extreme conditions in the northern portion.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extremely dry conditions remained in place across western Colorado in early July before monsoon rains brought some relief through the remainder of the month. The highest drought category D4 or exceptional drought conditions were realized across portions of northwest Colorado in the the first week of the month, with severe to extreme conditions in place else where across the western half of the state. The majority of reporting stations however did report above normal precipitation in July and significant improvement to the exceptional drought area was observed by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Portions of the upper Yampa river basin began July in exceptional drought conditions but above normal precipitation by the end of the month, lowered these drought conditions back to the extreme to severe categories.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rainfall produced flooding in Unaweep Canyon closing Highway 141 between mile markers 140 and 141 due to water over the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms dropped between 1 to 2 inches of rainfall over portions of Mesa County during the early evening hours of July 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced 1.3 inches of rain within 40 minutes near Powderhorn Ski Resort. Gusts up to 40 mph and some pea-sized hail also fell with these storms. The storm total precipitation at this location was 1.80 inches.  no significant flooding was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced heavy rainfall and minor flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall resulted in street flooding and some damage to businesses in the downtown area of Fruita.  Exactly an inch of rain fell within 45 minutes at one location. Radar estimated up to 1.5 inches of rainfall at other locations in Fruita.

October 2012 continued the string of below normal precipitation months across many of the climate stations in Western Colorado. The valley locations were short of normal of monthly normal precipitation by 0.75 to 1.25 over the majority of the locations while mountain stations were around 0.50 in deficit. For the most part severe to extreme drought persisted across the region with only a temporary improvement to moderate drought conditions over the central Colorado mountains at the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions remained in place along the central Colorado mountain ranges through the month of October.  However, the mountainous areas of eastern Gunnison County did improve slightly to the severe drought category by the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As western Colorado transitioned from fall to winter the storm track became more favorable for precipitation across the eastern Great Basin and northern Rockies, especially during the latter half of the month. Half of the climate reporting systems over western Colorado collected above normal precipitation for the first time in many months. The central and southern mountain ranges and adjacent valleys however continued to receive below normal precipitation through November.  Overall the precipitation was not enough to ease drought conditions and little change was seen in the severe to extreme categories in place across western Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions remained unchanged over the extreme southwest corner of Colorado during the month of December.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Damaging winds developed in and near the Front Range.  A peak wind gust to 104 mph was recorded in the foothills of Boulder County.  In Boulder, the high winds knocked down several trees, power poles and electrical lines.  Some of the fallen trees damaged homes and automobiles.  A semi-trailer was blown on its side along State Highway 93 near Marshall.  In Loveland, the strong winds downed power lines and caused scattered electrical outages, which affected approximately 150 residents.  In the mountains, the combination light to moderate snow driven by high winds, produced blizzard conditions above timberline.  Storm totals generally ranged from 3 to 8 inches.||Peak wind gusts included:  104 mph in south Boulder; 98 mph, 3 miles southwest of Pinecliffe; 95 mph, 2 miles northwest of Rocky Flats; 92 mph, along State Highway 93 near Marshall; 87 mph atop Berthoud Pass and in Boulder Canyon;  80 mph, 5 miles west-northwest of Boulder; 83 mph at NCAR Mesa Lab; 78 mph, 8 miles northeast of Four Corners; 79 mph at the National Wind Technology Center; 76 mph at Wondervu; 75 mph atop Loveland Pass and the NCAR Foothills Lab in Boulder; 74 mph at Blue Mountain, Boulder Municipal Airport, 9 miles east of Dillon and 1 mile northwest of Lyons; 73 mph, 4 miles east-northeast of Nederland; 72 mph at the Junction of State Highways 72 and 93; 64 mph in Loveland; 62 mph, 6 miles west-northwest of Berthoud and Superior; 61 mph at Erie Municipal Airport and the CSU Campus in Fort Collins; and 60 mph, 10 miles northeast of Pawnee Buttes.

For the most part severe to extreme drought persisted across the region with only a temporary improvement to moderate drought conditions over the central Colorado mountains at the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme to severe drought conditions remained in place across the northern Colorado  mountain ranges through the month of October.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As western Colorado transitioned from fall to winter the storm track became more favorable for precipitation across the eastern Great Basin and northern Rockies, especially during the latter half of the month. Half of the climate reporting systems over western Colorado collected above normal precipitation for the first time in many months. The central and southern mountain ranges and adjacent valleys however continued to receive below normal precipitation through November.  Overall the precipitation was not enough to ease drought conditions and little change was seen in the severe to extreme categories in place across western Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe to extreme drought conditions remained unchanged over the upper Gunnison River valley through the month of December.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extremely dry conditions remained in place across western Colorado in early July before monsoon rains brought some relief through the remainder of the month. The highest drought category D4 or exceptional drought conditions were realized across portions of northwest Colorado in the the first week of the month, with severe to extreme conditions in place else where across the western half of the state. The majority of reporting stations however did report above normal precipitation in July and significant improvement to the exceptional drought area was observed by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe to extreme drought conditions remained in place across the northern San Juan mountain range. July rainfall amounts were above normal over the region by the end of the month but not enough to ease the persistent dry conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Damaging winds developed in and near the Front Range.  A peak wind gust to 104 mph was recorded in the foothills of Boulder County.  In Boulder, the high winds knocked down several trees, power poles and electrical lines.  Some of the fallen trees damaged homes and automobiles.  A semi-trailer was blown on its side along State Highway 93 near Marshall.  In Loveland, the strong winds downed power lines and caused scattered electrical outages, which affected approximately 150 residents.  In the mountains, the combination light to moderate snow driven by high winds, produced blizzard conditions above timberline.  Storm totals generally ranged from 3 to 8 inches.||Peak wind gusts included:  104 mph in south Boulder; 98 mph, 3 miles southwest of Pinecliffe; 95 mph, 2 miles northwest of Rocky Flats; 92 mph, along State Highway 93 near Marshall; 87 mph atop Berthoud Pass and in Boulder Canyon;  80 mph, 5 miles west-northwest of Boulder; 83 mph at NCAR Mesa Lab; 78 mph, 8 miles northeast of Four Corners; 79 mph at the National Wind Technology Center; 76 mph at Wondervu; 75 mph atop Loveland Pass and the NCAR Foothills Lab in Boulder; 74 mph at Blue Mountain, Boulder Municipal Airport, 9 miles east of Dillon and 1 mile northwest of Lyons; 73 mph, 4 miles east-northeast of Nederland; 72 mph at the Junction of State Highways 72 and 93; 64 mph in Loveland; 62 mph, 6 miles west-northwest of Berthoud and Superior; 61 mph at Erie Municipal Airport and the CSU Campus in Fort Collins; and 60 mph, 10 miles northeast of Pawnee Buttes.

Extreme to severe drought conditions were held on place across western Colorado in August. Slight improvement was seen across portions of the central and southern mountains due to the impact of monsoonal moisture. However, the majority of climate reporting stations received monthly precipitation totals well below normal, and most of these were at least three quarters of an inch below normal. The wildland fire danger remained high during August but the main impacts were felt in the agricultural sector due to crop and pasture land losses.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought conditions remained in place over the Grand and Battlement Mesa areas as monthly precipitation totals remained below normal in August.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Vallecito fire was started by lightning during the late afternoon hours on October 16th. This wildfire eventually grew to 1500 acres through early November before a late fall snow storm ended the growth of the fire. This fire was active near and inside the perimeter of the Missionary Ridge fire of 2002. Due to snags in this area and steep terrain the fire was not directly attacked. No structures were lost or evacuations needed for this fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This lightning caused wildfire started October 16th and persisted through an early November snow storm. The fire grew to approximately 1500 acres, with much of this inside the Missionary Ridge wildfire perimeter of 2002. Numerous snags and steep terrain did not allow for a direct attack of the fire, but no structures were lost or evacuations issued by the time the fire was extinguished.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extremely dry conditions remained in place across western Colorado in early July before monsoon rains brought some relief through the remainder of the month. The highest drought category D4 or exceptional drought conditions were realized across portions of northwest Colorado in the the first week of the month, with severe to extreme conditions in place else where across the western half of the state. The majority of reporting stations however did report above normal precipitation in July and significant improvement to the exceptional drought area was observed by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Portions of the lower Yampa river basin began July in exceptional drought conditions but above normal precipitation by the end of the month, eased these drought conditions slightly back to the extreme category.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved across northwest Colorado on the afternoon of July 6th, producing very heavy rainfall. Numerous reports of over an inch of rainfall were received from the Steamboat Springs area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall in excess of one and a third inches was reported within about a 30 minute period.  Only minor flooding was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A heavy rainfall producing thunderstorm produced flash flooding over the Pine Ridge burn scar.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm with heavy rainfall moved over the Pine Ridge burn scar and produced significant runoff and flash flooding in a remote area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pilot reported a brief funnel cloud below a thunderstorm in the Durango area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was photographed by a pilot who was at the Val Air gliderport.  The funnel cloud was located to the west of the gliderport over the Rockridge subdivision and appeared to last around 5 minutes.  It extended 300 to 400 feet below the cumulonimbus cloud base.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As western Colorado transitioned from fall to winter the storm track became more favorable for precipitation across the eastern Great Basin and northern Rockies, especially during the latter half of the month. Half of the climate reporting systems over western Colorado collected above normal precipitation for the first time in many months. The central and southern mountain ranges and adjacent valleys however continued to receive below normal precipitation through November.  Overall the precipitation was not enough to ease drought conditions and little change was seen in the severe to extreme categories in place across western Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought conditions remained unchanged across the lower Yampa River basin and the northwest Colorado plateau region through December.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extremely dry conditions remained in place across western Colorado in early July before monsoon rains brought some relief through the remainder of the month. The highest drought category D4 or exceptional drought conditions were realized across portions of northwest Colorado in the the first week of the month, with severe to extreme conditions in place else where across the western half of the state. The majority of reporting stations however did report above normal precipitation in July and significant improvement to the exceptional drought area was observed by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Portions of the central Yampa river basin began July in exceptional drought conditions but above normal precipitation by the end of the month, lowered these drought conditions back to the extreme to severe categories.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extremely dry conditions remained in place across western Colorado in early July before monsoon rains brought some relief through the remainder of the month. The highest drought category D4 or exceptional drought conditions were realized across portions of northwest Colorado in the the first week of the month, with severe to extreme conditions in place else where across the western half of the state. The majority of reporting stations however did report above normal precipitation in July and significant improvement to the exceptional drought area was observed by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Portions of the Elkhead and Park mountain ranges began July in exceptional drought conditions but above normal precipitation by the end of the month, lowered drought conditions back to the severe category.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The trend of monthly below normal precipitation continued into the month of September across western Colorado. All climate reporting stations across the western half of the state reported readings of around a half to an inch below normal with little help from a weakening monsoonal flow. Little change in drought conditions were noted with severe to extreme drought levels remaining in place throughout the month. The wildland fire danger remained high during September but the main impacts were felt in the agricultural sector due to crop and pasture land losses.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme to severe drought conditions remained in place across the upper portions of the Gunnison River valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with very heavy rainfall rates and small hail moved through La Plata County during the early evening hours of July 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime sized hail and 0.84 inches of rainfall fell in 20 minutes east of Ignacio. The storm total precipitation was 1.04 inches.  No significant flooding was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rainfall moved through eastern Delta County during the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over 0.75 inches of rain fell within an hour from a thunderstorm that moved between Paonia and Crawford Reservoir.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Powell and Little Duck fires were ignited by lightning and burned 900 acres on public and private lands during the middle part of July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Powell wildfire complex consisted of two fires, the Powell and the Little Duck, which were ignited by lightning on July 17th about 5 miles northwest of Meeker. These fires burned approximately 900 acres before containment and no structures were threatened.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high alpine wind sensor reported a wind gust of 84 mph as thunderstorms moved through southwest Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Eagle Mountain weather station measured thunderstorm outflow wind gusts of 84 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moving across western Colorado produced areas of heavy rainfall, with flooding being reported in Mesa County. Outflow winds from these storms also produced damage in Pitkin County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm wind gusts downed a tree at Avalanche Road near Redstone, blocking both lanes of Highway 133.

CZ_NAME: S & SE GRAND / W CENTRAL & SW BOULDER / GILPIN / CLEAR CREEK / SUMMIT / N & W PARK COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A skier was killed when he triggered a small avalanche on the Mary Jane side of the Winter Park ski resort.  He was skiing inbounds when the accident occurred and was found completely buried in two to three feet of snow.  The Ski Patrol found the man unresponsive in the trees near the Trestle trail and began CPR when they found him.  The victim was taken to the emergency room at the base of Winter Park and later died due to asphyxiation.  This was the second avalanche in Colorado since 1991 to kill a skier in clearly open terrain within a ski area boundary.

Several small clusters of thunderstorms formed across Kit Carson and Yuma counties in Colorado during the evening hours and moved slowly southeast.  The storms in Yuma county produced moderate to heavy rainfall, but pea size hail accumulated to a depth 12-18 inches just west of Idalia on Highway 36. County road graders had to be called out to clear the hail.  One report of golf ball sized hail was also received.  Weaker storms south of Interstate 70 collapsed and produced a severe wind gust to 59 mph at Burlington airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The largest hail was about the size of a penny.  However, pea sized hail accumulated to a depth of 12 to 18 inches west of Idalia on Highway 36.  Snow plows had to be called out after CDOT received reports of two vehicles getting stuck in the hail.  The hail swath affected 4 miles of highway 36 but extended further to the NW and SE over open fields and county roads.

CZ_NAME: S & SE GRAND / W CENTRAL & SW BOULDER / GILPIN / CLEAR CREEK / SUMMIT / N & W PARK COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level jet stream brought a prolonged period of moderate to heavy banded snow to portions of the North Central Mountains.  Storm totals in the mountains of western Jackson and western Grand Counties ranged from 14 to 24 inches...with 6 to 12 inches elsewhere.  Peak wind gusts to around 75 mph atop Berthoud and Loveland Passes produced blizzard conditions at times in the higher exposed areas.  Several accidents, some serious, forced the closure of Interstate 70 west of Denver to Vail for several hours.

CZ_NAME: JEFFERSON & W DOUGLAS COUNTIES ABOVE 6000 FEET / GILPIN / CLEAR CREEK / NE PARK COUNTIES BELOW 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong southwest winds ahead of an approaching cold front produced high to extreme fire danger across the Front Range Foothills and Palmer Divide.   As a result, a 50-acre prescribed burn that had been conducted the previous week reignited in the foothills of Jefferson County, southwest of Denver.  The strong wind gusts carried embers from the interior of the burn area, across containment lines and into very dry fuels which initiated the wildfire. It then spread into the crowns of the trees and driven by the strong winds, quickly advanced to the northeast onto private lands.  Local firefighters immediately responded to the wildfire, but were unable to contain it, due to the extreme winds and dry and abundant fuels.  ||The combination of very strong winds, record warm temperatures and extremely dry conditions for month of March; all contributed to a rapid increase in fire growth during the afternoon of March 26th.  A total of 900 homes were evacuated on the 26th.  The wildfire scorched a total of 4,150 acres; it destroyed 27 homes and resulted in the deaths of three local residents.   The property damage alone was estimated to be $11 million.  The Lower North Fork wildfire, south of Conifer, was not 100 percent contained until April 2nd.  Peak wind gusts included:  70 mph, 1 mile north of Strasburg; 66 mph, 1 mile northwest of Golden; 62 mph at Cedar Point; and 56 mph at Denver International Airport. Officially in Denver, March 2012 was the driest and least snowiest on record.

CZ_NAME: JEFFERSON & W DOUGLAS COUNTIES ABOVE 6000 FEET / GILPIN / CLEAR CREEK / NE PARK COUNTIES BELOW 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong southwest winds ahead of an approaching cold front produced high to extreme fire danger across the Front Range Foothills and Palmer Divide.   As a result, a 50-acre prescribed burn that had been conducted the previous week reignited in the foothills of Jefferson County, southwest of Denver.  The strong wind gusts carried embers from the interior of the burn area, across containment lines and into very dry fuels which initiated the wildfire. It then spread into the crowns of the trees and driven by the strong winds, quickly advanced to the northeast onto private lands.  Local firefighters immediately responded to the wildfire, but were unable to contain it, due to the extreme winds and dry and abundant fuels.  ||The combination of very strong winds, record warm temperatures and extremely dry conditions for month of March; all contributed to a rapid increase in fire growth during the afternoon of March 26th.  A total of 900 homes were evacuated on the 26th.  The wildfire scorched a total of 4,150 acres; it destroyed 27 homes and resulted in the deaths of three local residents.   The property damage alone was estimated to be $11 million.  The Lower North Fork wildfire, south of Conifer, was not 100 percent contained until April 2nd.  Peak wind gusts included:  70 mph, 1 mile north of Strasburg; 66 mph, 1 mile northwest of Golden; 62 mph at Cedar Point; and 56 mph at Denver International Airport. Officially in Denver, March 2012 was the driest and least snowiest on record.

CZ_NAME: N & NE ELBERT COUNTY BELOW 6000 FEET / N LINCOLN COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong southwest winds ahead of an approaching cold front produced high to extreme fire danger across the Front Range Foothills and Palmer Divide.   As a result, a 50-acre prescribed burn that had been conducted the previous week reignited in the foothills of Jefferson County, southwest of Denver.  The strong wind gusts carried embers from the interior of the burn area, across containment lines and into very dry fuels which initiated the wildfire. It then spread into the crowns of the trees and driven by the strong winds, quickly advanced to the northeast onto private lands.  Local firefighters immediately responded to the wildfire, but were unable to contain it, due to the extreme winds and dry and abundant fuels.  ||The combination of very strong winds, record warm temperatures and extremely dry conditions for month of March; all contributed to a rapid increase in fire growth during the afternoon of March 26th.  A total of 900 homes were evacuated on the 26th.  The wildfire scorched a total of 4,150 acres; it destroyed 27 homes and resulted in the deaths of three local residents.   The property damage alone was estimated to be $11 million.  The Lower North Fork wildfire, south of Conifer, was not 100 percent contained until April 2nd.  Peak wind gusts included:  70 mph, 1 mile north of Strasburg; 66 mph, 1 mile northwest of Golden; 62 mph at Cedar Point; and 56 mph at Denver International Airport. Officially in Denver, March 2012 was the driest and least snowiest on record.

Several small clusters of thunderstorms formed across Kit Carson and Yuma counties in Colorado during the evening hours and moved slowly southeast.  The storms in Yuma county produced moderate to heavy rainfall, but pea size hail accumulated to a depth 12-18 inches just west of Idalia on Highway 36. County road graders had to be called out to clear the hail.  One report of golf ball sized hail was also received.  Weaker storms south of Interstate 70 collapsed and produced a severe wind gust to 59 mph at Burlington airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A collapsing thunderstorm resulted in a marginally severe wind gust form the south of 59 mph. It was measured by ASOS at Kit Carson County airport south of Burlington.

CZ_NAME: S & E JACKSON / LARIMER / N & NE GRAND / NW BOULDER COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving and powerful storm system brought heavy snow to areas in and near the Front Range Foothills, with blizzard conditions over the northeastern plains of Colorado.  In the Front Range Foothills, the snow piled up to over 4 feet in some areas.  Across the Palmer Divide, the combination of snow and gusty winds resulted in road closures with snow drifts ranging from 2 to 5 feet in depth.  Northerly winds 15 to 25 mph were common with gusts to 40 mph.   Several snowfall records were also set in Denver.  At Denver International Airport, 12.5 inches of snow feet on the 3rd shattered the previous record of 7.5 inches for the date.  It also set a new daily record for the entire month of February.  A new 3-day record was also established for Denver.  The 3-day storm total from February 2nd to the 4th was 15.9 inches, which broke the previous record of 14.1 inches in 1912.  In contrast, storm totals generally ranged from 1 to 3 inches in the mountains west of the Continental Divide.||Denver International Airport canceled more than six hundred flights.  In addition, snow and blowing snow produced near zero visibilities, forcing officials to close the westbound lanes of Interstate 70, between the Kansas state line and Denver, as well as the eastbound lanes from Denver to Limon.  Other road closures included:  State Highway 86, between Kiowa and I-70, U.S. Highway 40, between Limon and Eads, and State Highway 71, from Last Chance to Limon to Ordway.||Storm totals along the Front Range Mountains and Foothills included:  51 inches at Coal Creek Canyon, 45.5 inches, 4.6 miles northeast of Ward; 44.5 inches, 3 miles west of Jamestown; 38 inches, 3 miles north of Blackhawk; 37 inches, 3 miles west-southwest of Conifer and 4 miles east-northeast of Nederland; 35.5 inches, 3.6 miles west-northwest of Boulder; 34 inches, 5.2 miles east-southeast of Aspen Springs; 33 inches near Evergreen, 32 inches at Genesee; 31 inches, 10.3 miles west of Bellvue and Eldora Mountain Ski Resort; 30 inches, 10.6 miles west of Livermore; 28 inches; 3.2 miles north-northwest of Horsetooth Mountain; 27 inches at Deadman Hill; 24 inches at Echo Mountain Ski Resort; 21 inches at Niwot Ridge SNOTEL; 19 inches at Gross Reservoir;  15 inches at Bear Lake State Park; with 14 inches near Estes Park and Glen Haven.||Along the Urban Corridor storm totals included:  22 inches in Broomfield; 21 inches at Lafayette, Louisville and Westminster; 20 inches at Northglenn; 19 inches at the National Weather Service in Boulder, Castle Rock, Centenniel and Parker;  18 inches in Arvada, 16.5 inches in Erie, 13.5 inches near Longmont; 11.5 inches in Fort Collins and Loveland; 11 inches in Lyons and 10 inches in Frederick.||Along the Palmer Divide storm totals included:  26 inches, 14 miles east-northeast of Kiowa; 25 inches, 10 miles south-southwest of Buckley Air Force Base, and 8 miles southeast of Watkins; 20 inches near Strasburg; 16 inches at Agate, 12 inches near Elizabeth; and 8 inches, 5 miles south-southeast of Sedalia.  ||Across the northeast plains of Colorado, storm totals included:  13 inches, 11 miles east-southeast of Holyoke; 11 inches, 4 miles north of Arriba; 10 inches, 6.4 miles west-northwest of Otis and Woodrow; 9.5 inches near Amherst; 6.5 inches in Sterling; 6 inches in Brush; with 5.5 inches in Karval.

A Pacific trough brought heavy snow accumulations and strong winds to many mountain areas, as well as significant snowfall to some lower elevation areas of western Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulations of 4 to 12 inches were measured across the area.  Wind gusts of 25 to 45 mph produced areas of blowing and drifting snow with localized blizzard conditions.  Locally stronger wind gusts included 56 mph near Monarch Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous Pacific trough produced heavy snowfall to portions of western Colorado.  This storm event began to impact western Colorado late on February 27th and lingered into the early morning areas of February 29th at some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulations of 5 to 12 inches were measured across the area. Locally heavier amounts included 15 inches at a house located 15 miles west of Montrose, and 15 inches at the Columbine Pass SNOTEL site.  Wind gusts of 35 to 50 mph produced localized blizzard conditions and snow drifts 2 to 3 feet deep.  Highway 62 over Dallas Divide was closed for a period of time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions remained in a steady state across much of western Colorado in February as most climate stations reported near to slightly above normal precipitation amounts during the month. Although locations in the northwest, Grand Valley and central mountains received below normal precipitation amounts, for the most part drought conditions did not expand or worsen.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate drought conditions remained in place across the high valleys of northwest Colorado through February.

CZ_NAME: S & E JACKSON / LARIMER / N & NE GRAND / NW BOULDER COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level jet stream produced high winds in and near the Front Range mountains, foothills and adjacent plains of northeast Colorado.  In Boulder, the strong winds snapped power poles and toppled trees.  As a result, about 7,000 Xcel Energy customers were without power for several hours.  The high winds overturned a tractor-trailer on Foothills Highway overpass, north of Valmont Road.  The driver suffered minor injuries.  A parked car was totalled when it was crushed by a fallen tree near the University of Boulder.  The strong winds were also responsible for two Boulder County wildfires which consumed a total of 65 acres.  Colorado Department of Transportation officials closed U.S. 36 at McCaslin Boulevard in both directions after the wind caused damage to the pedestrian overpass.  Metal siding from the overpass fell into highway; fortunately there were no injuries.    ||In total, electrical outages affected 46,000 customers along the Front Range.  Damage to roofs, siding, garage doors and fences was also reported along the Urban Corridor.  In Timnath, the wind knocked a power line on to the roof of the Colorado Feed and Grain building.  The ensuing fire partially destroyed the building and forced the evacuation of three people.  Across the northeast plains of Colorado the stronger wind gusts were more localized and over a much shorter duration.   ||Peak wind gusts included:  93 mph near Lyons; 88 mph at NCAR Mesa Lab; 85 mph, 2 miles southwest of Rocky Flats; 82 mph, 5 miles northwest of Boulder; 81 mph, 2 miles north of Longmont, 4 miles east-northeast of Nederland, the National Wind Technology Center and near Wondervu; 80 mph at the Junction of Highways 72 and 93; 79 mph at the Boulder Municipal Airport; 78 mph, 6 miles northwest of Boulder and atop Loveland Pass; 77 mph at Rocky Mountain Municipal Airport; 76 mph atop Berthoud Pass; 75 mph near Aspen Springs; 73 mph, 5 miles northeast of Hoyt; 70 mph at Longmont Municipal Airport; 64 mph at Erie Municipal Airport; 63 mph at Sterling; 62 mph, 8 miles south-southwest of Grover; and 60 mph, 3 miles west of New Raymer.

Damage to roofs, siding, garage doors and fences was also reported along the Urban Corridor.  In Timnath, the wind knocked a power line on to the roof of the Colorado Feed and Grain building.  The ensuing fire partially destroyed the building and forced the evacuation of three people.  Across the northeast plains of Colorado the stronger wind gusts were more localized and over a much shorter duration.   ||Peak wind gusts included:  93 mph near Lyons; 88 mph at NCAR Mesa Lab; 85 mph, 2 miles southwest of Rocky Flats; 82 mph, 5 miles northwest of Boulder; 81 mph, 2 miles north of Longmont, 4 miles east-northeast of Nederland, the National Wind Technology Center and near Wondervu; 80 mph at the Junction of Highways 72 and 93; 79 mph at the Boulder Municipal Airport; 78 mph, 6 miles northwest of Boulder and atop Loveland Pass; 77 mph at Rocky Mountain Municipal Airport; 76 mph atop Berthoud Pass; 75 mph near Aspen Springs; 73 mph, 5 miles northeast of Hoyt; 70 mph at Longmont Municipal Airport; 64 mph at Erie Municipal Airport; 63 mph at Sterling; 62 mph, 8 miles south-southwest of Grover; and 60 mph, 3 miles west of New Raymer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Little Sand wildfire was started 13 miles northwest of Pagosa Springs on May 13th by lightning. This fire burned 24,900 acres along the Piedra River and Weminuche valleys through mid July, before being considered fully contained. Structures were threatened during this time frame but none were lost.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Little Sand wildfire was started May 13th northwest of Pagosa Springs and burned 24,900 acres along and above the Piedra and Weminuche valleys. Twenty structures, including outbuldings, were threatened but not lost during this fire. Several road and recreational areas were closed due to the proximity of the fire. During the early stages of this fire, nightly down-valley winds created air quality problems along the valleys and the Highway 160 corridor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding in and near the Front Range Foothills.  In central Larimer County, heavy rain from nearly stationary thunderstorms caused flash flooding in the High Park burn scar.  The heavy rain caused debris flows of rock, mud and ash onto the roadway, which forced the closure of State Highway 14, between Stove Praire and Ted's Place.  Rist Canyon was also closed briefly near Stove Praire.  The mudslides ranged in depth from 1 to 3 feet.  The more significant debris was reported on the Hewlett side of High Park.  The heavy rain also flooded numerous basements across Larimer County.  In a neighborhood west of Horsetooth Reservoir, water created a debris dam in a drainage area, which backed into a nearby garage, basement and crawlspace.  In Wellington, some basements were extensively flooded; one was filled with 8 feet of brown, muddy water.||In Boulder County, heavy rainfall caused flooding problems near Jamestown.  James Canyon Drive was closed after heavy rain washed out a section of the roadway.   Heavy rain washed out a section of Magnolia Road, east-southeast of Nederland.  Nearby, a trained spotter 4 miles east-northeast of Nederland, measured 2.20 inches of rainfall. Flash flooding was also reported in the Fourmile burn area along Summerville Road.  In Dacono, in southern Weld County, the Colorado Department of Transportation used snowplows to clear standing water, up to 6 inches deep, from a section of Interstate 25.  The interstate was closed in both directions for nearly two hours.  South of the Denver area, in central Douglas County, flash flooding was reported near Perry Park, where 2.5 inches of rain fell in one hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding and mudslides in parts of the central Larimer County, including the High Park burn scar.

CZ_NAME: PARADOX VALLEY / LOWER DOLORES RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent drought across southwest Colorado through the winter and spring months of early 2012, led to conditions favorable for large wildfire growth. On the afternoon of May 25th, a human caused fire started in the Paradox valley. This wildfire eventually burned across 5742 acres of federal land and threatened around 25 structures before being fully contained on June 3rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sunrise Mine wildfire which was human caused started on the afternoon of May 25th near Paradox. Conditions were favorable for large wildfire growth and this fire eventually burned over 5700 acres before being fully contained on June 3rd. Ten residences and fifteen other buildings were threatened during this wildfire but none were lost.

Heavy rain occurred over a large portion of the Waldo Canyon burn scar from late afternoon through early morning.  A flow of ash and gravel covered US Highway 24 to a depth of five feet, stranding a truck and trailer.  Debris flowed down Sand Gulch into the playground of the Ute Pass Elementary School and into two houses on the other side of Chipita Park Road.  Debris in Wellington Gulch roared through a campground, which sustained damage to buildings and the property. Heavy damage was done to Colorado Springs Utilities roadways and a backup water pipeline around Nichols and Northfield Reservoirs. Additionally, flashing flooding occurred in the Security-Widefield area, where many streets and intersections were flooded. A women in her car was swept 500-feet downstream in the flood waters of Jimmy Camp Creek at the Peaceful Valley Road crossing, and was rescued.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Weber wildfire started June 22 at approximately 4:15 pm, 6 miles south of Mancos and was determined to be human caused. Several areas within or near the fire perimeter were evacuated and numerous road closures were necessary. This fire burned 10,133 acres until containment on July 6th, with both private and public land being affected.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Weber fire was deemed to be human caused and burned a total of 10,133 acres from late June to early July.  Several subdivisions near Mancos were evacuated and nearby roads closed during the height of this fire. Several miles of fence posts and one  outbuilding were burned.  A communications tower was temporarily off line as power to the facility was interrupted by the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms brought damaging wind and hail, heavy rain, along several tornadoes, one of which was rated an EF-2.  The storms produced hail from 1.5 to 2.75 inches in diameter.  In addition to the large hail, heavy rainfall from 1 to 2 inches also accompanied the storms.  The combination of hail and heavy rain caused extensive street flooding across Aurora, Castle Rock, Centennial, Cherry Creek, Englewood, South Denver, Highlands Ranch, Lakewood and Littleton.  The hail was reportedly knee deep in several areas making roads impassable.  As a result, snow plows had to be summoned to clear the streets.  In Castle Rock, a King Soopers supermarket sustained extensive damage when roof partially collapsed under the weight of the hail.  Total property damage estimates along the Front Range for the 6th and 7th combined was 321.1 million dollars. ||A tornado touched down in Elbert County Thursday evening, June 7th, producing considerable damage to homes and several farm buildings in south central Elbert County.  The tornado was rated an EF-2 at it's strongest point near Elbert County Road 82 and just west of Elbert County Road 97.  The tornado initially touched down approximately at County Road 101 about 1 mile north of County Road 90, and then traveled south-southwest into El Paso County.  It was approximately one half mile wide at one point and produced a debris width of 1.5 miles.  One minor injury occurred due to broken glass.  Two other tornadoes touched down but did no damage.   In Elbert County alone, at least 136 homes were damaged; 32 sustained moderate to severe damage.  ||Severe thunderstorms also produced large hail and damaging winds across parts of Larimer, Weld and Morgan Counties.   In Weld County, a flash flood washed out a section of State Highway 392 just east of Lucerne.  During the storm, a culvert underneath the road was washed out in addition to a section of the roadway, approximately a 30 foot by 30 foot section.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down and produced moderate to severe damage to numerous homes and other farm buildings in southern Elbert County. One person suffered minor injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A brief but powerful windstorm associated with a fast moving cold front swept from northwest to southeast across the Urban Corridor and adjacent plains during in the evening.  Peak wind gusts ranging from 58 to 71 mph downed trees and power lines which damaged homes and vehicles.  Several temporary structures were also damaged.  Approximately 50,000 people were without power in the Denver, Fort Collins and Greeley areas.  Some schools were closed the following day until power could be restored.     ||Peak wind gusts included:  71 mph at Fort Collins, 69 mph in Golden, 64 mph at Littleton and Sterling Municipal Airport, 63 mph in Loveland, 62 mph at Buckley AFB, in Denver, at Walnut St. and I-25, and Longmont; 59 mph at Centennial Airport, 58 mph at Denver City Park, Highlands Ranch and Keenseburg; 57 mph at Wiggins and 55 mph in Greeley.  Atop Loveland Pass, a peak wind gust to 89 mph was recorded.

Strong winds developed over extreme Northeast Colorado as a low pressure system intensified over the Central Plains.  The intense winds produced near zero visibilities in blowing dust, 4 miles east of Haxtun.  Trained spotters in Crook and Holyoke both recorded a peak wind gust to 64 mph.  Trees were also downed in Holyoke.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Wolf wildfire was started by lightning 5 miles southeast of Elk Springs Colorado during the afternoon hours on August 4th. The fire persisted through August ninth and consumed 6100 acres of public and private land.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wolf wildfire was started by lightning in the open lands of central Moffat County during the afternoon of August 4th. This fire consumed 6100 acres of public and private land.  The fire also threatened oil and gas facilities three miles south of the incident, as well as critical wildlife habit and grazing range land.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Little Sand wildfire was started by lightning northwest of Pagosa Springs in mid May. This fire continued to spread northward through the early weeks of July and eventually scorched 24,900 acres along the Piedra River and Weminuche valleys until being deemed fully contained by July 27th. Structures were threatened during this time frame but none were lost.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Little Sand wildfire was started May 13th northwest of Pagosa Springs and burned 24,900 acres along and above the Piedra and Weminuche valleys. Twenty structures, including outbuildings, were threatened but not lost during this fire. Several road and recreational areas were closed due to the proximity of the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lee side surface trough set up across Colorado producing a zone of strong south to southwest surface winds over much of the region.  Wind gusts from 55 to 70 mph occurred over portions of far eastern Colorado and northwest Kansas during the day. |A peak wind of 68 mph occurred at Kirk, Colorado in Yuma County, with a gust of 58 mph reported at a mesonet station west of Goodland, Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 68 mph was measured at a Kirk, Colorado mesonet site. Downed live electric wires or arcing resulted in a large grass fire which destroyed several residences, as well as damage to agricultural equipment.  Thousands of acres of grassland and pastures were burned.  Three volunteer firefighters were injured fighting the fire.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds over 40 mph were measured at Kit Carson County airport south of Burlington.  In addition, numerous gusts to 55 mph occurred across Kit Carson County through the day.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wildfire raced north across Yuma County in 50 to 70 mph winds, destroying two farmsteads and injuring three firefighters.  An estimated 2400 acres of grassland and range land was burned.  The fire is thought to have started around 1:15 pm MDT resulting from arcing utility wires.  The fire was 90% contained by 7:00 am MDT the following morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Pine Ridge wildfire started on the afternoon of June 27th by lightning above the Book Cliffs, approximately 10 miles northeast of Grand Junction. The fire burned nearly 14,000 acres from late June to early July on both public and private lands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pine Ridge fire was started by lightning June 27th and continued to burn until fully contained on the afternoon of July 4th. The fire threatened numerous resource concerns, such as oil and gas well locations, cultural resources, threatened and endangered species, and the Wild Horse range. However, the only losses were to three unoccupied tents and two camper units.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Pine Ridge wildfire started on the afternoon of June 27th by lightning above the Book Cliffs, approximately 10 miles northeast of Grand Junction. The fire burned nearly 14,000 acres from late June to early July on both public and private lands.

However, the only losses were to three unoccupied tents and two camper units. The fire was halted within a mile of a farm residence near De Beque. Interstate 70 and the local rail line were temporarily closed during the height of fire activity in late June.

Moderate to severe drought conditions covered all of western Colorado by the end of May, with extreme drought conditions developing across the northwest portion of the state. A historically reduced snow pack and resultant below normal runoff, below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures all contributed to the increase in elevated drought conditions across the region. An increase in wildland fire danger was the main impact from the drought conditions in the late Spring period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions remained in place across the lower reaches of the Gunnison and  Colorado River basins as spring precipitation remained well below normal and runoff from the snow pack was greatly reduced.

Moderate to severe drought conditions covered all of western Colorado by the end of May, with extreme drought conditions developing across the northwest portion of the state. A historically reduced snow pack and resultant below normal runoff, below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures all contributed to the increase in elevated drought conditions across the region. An increase in wildland fire danger was the main impact from the drought conditions in the late Spring period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions remained in place across much of the central mountain areas of western Colorado. However a small portion of extreme drought moved into the northwest corner of the area by the end of May.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to severe drought conditions covered all of western Colorado by the end of May, with extreme drought conditions developing across the northwest portion of the state. A historically reduced snow pack and resultant below normal runoff, below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures all contributed to the increase in elevated drought conditions across the region. An increase in wildland fire danger was the main impact from the drought conditions in the late Spring period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions persisted across the southern portions of the Grand Mesa and an area of extreme drought developed across Battlement Mesa and the remainder of the Grand Mesa in May as precipitation remained well below normal and a limited snow pack kept runoff at a minimum.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms brought damaging wind and hail, heavy rain, along several tornadoes, one of which was rated an EF-2.  The storms produced hail from 1.5 to 2.75 inches in diameter.  In addition to the large hail, heavy rainfall from 1 to 2 inches also accompanied the storms.  The combination of hail and heavy rain caused extensive street flooding across Aurora, Castle Rock, Centennial, Cherry Creek, Englewood, South Denver, Highlands Ranch, Lakewood and Littleton.  The hail was reportedly knee deep in several areas making roads impassable.  As a result, snow plows had to be summoned to clear the streets.  In Castle Rock, a King Soopers supermarket sustained extensive damage when roof partially collapsed under the weight of the hail.  Total property damage estimates along the Front Range for the 6th and 7th combined was 321.1 million dollars. ||A tornado touched down in Elbert County Thursday evening, June 7th, producing considerable damage to homes and several farm buildings in south central Elbert County.  The tornado was rated an EF-2 at it's strongest point near Elbert County Road 82 and just west of Elbert County Road 97.  The tornado initially touched down approximately at County Road 101 about 1 mile north of County Road 90, and then traveled south-southwest into El Paso County.  It was approximately one half mile wide at one point and produced a debris width of 1.5 miles.  One minor injury occurred due to broken glass.  Two other tornadoes touched down but did no damage.   In Elbert County alone, at least 136 homes were damaged; 32 sustained moderate to severe damage.  ||Severe thunderstorms also produced large hail and damaging winds across parts of Larimer, Weld and Morgan Counties.   In Weld County, a flash flood washed out a section of State Highway 392 just east of Lucerne.  During the storm, a culvert underneath the road was washed out in addition to a section of the roadway, approximately a 30 foot by 30 foot section.

Moderate to severe drought conditions covered all of western Colorado by the end of May, with extreme drought conditions developing across the northwest portion of the state. A historically reduced snow pack and resultant below normal runoff, below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures all contributed to the increase in elevated drought conditions across the region. An increase in wildland fire danger was the main impact from the drought conditions in the late Spring period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions transitioned to extreme drought conditions across the upper Yampa River basin in May as precipitation remained well below normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms broke late in the evening, striking areas hardest from Denver southward.  Locations impacted by the storms included but were not limited to:  Aurora, Castle Rock, Centennial, Highlands Ranch, Lone Tree, Parker, Surrey Ridge.  The storms produced a barrage of large hail, damaging straight line winds, flash flooding and several short lived tornadoes.  The hail ranged in size from 1 to 2 inches in diameter, and caused extensive damage to homes and automobiles.  The hail inundated the roadways with several inches of hail in Douglas County.  Consequently, snow plows had to be called out to clear the roadways. One tornado that touched down near Grover in Weld County ripped a tree from the ground and tossed it approximately twenty feet.  ||The combination of torrential hail and heavy rain produced flash flooding in parts of Elbert, Douglas and Arapahoe Counties, as thunderstorms brought up to 3.35 inches of rain to some areas within 90 minutes.  In Aurora, Picadilly Road was closed from flooding north of 6th Avenue.  A water rescue took place on South Gun Club Road in Arapahoe County, where floodwaters were rushing to depth of 3 feet.  Flash flooding forced the closure of several streets and roads from Parker south to The Pinery, where the floodwaters inundated the roadway with up to 2 feet in several locations.||At Centennial Airport in Arapahoe County, a historic B-17 Flying Fortress suffered extensive damage as hailstones as large as ping pong balls hit the aircraft. Although the airframe itself did not require repair, the fabric-covered ailerons and elevators were extensively damaged.  The hail came straight down and punched holes in the fabric-covered control surfaces.  The plane landed just hours before the storm hit to participate in the weekend tour stop.  Lightning struck two homes, one in Lakewood and the other in Parker.  Straight line winds downed trees and power lines in Aurora.  As a result, scattered electrical outages affect approximately five thousand residents.

Moderate to severe drought conditions covered all of western Colorado by the end of May, with extreme drought conditions developing across the northwest portion of the state. A historically reduced snow pack and resultant below normal runoff, below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures all contributed to the increase in elevated drought conditions across the region. An increase in wildland fire danger was the main impact from the drought conditions in the late Spring period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions transitioned to extreme drought conditions across the lower Yampa River basin in May as precipitation remained well below normal.
